fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this geospatial coordinate toolkit we've been talking about. Basically devs are spending way too much time re-implementing the same projection math and distance calculations over and over, and we keep getting bug reports about coordinate axis mixups and inconsistent rounding in distance outputs. The idea is a clean CLI tool that reads a JSON command from stdin and writes results to stdout — similar in spirit to the approach we used in that data pipeline adapter project last quarter (you know the one).

The tool needs to handle stuff like: converting coordinates between different spatial reference systems, measuring distances along the earth's surface, looking up metadata about map projections and units, filtering some kind of grid file catalog, and also doing file checksum verification. Error cases should come back as plain text error categories, not raw Python exceptions — our downstream consumers really can't parse stack traces.

The codebase should be properly split up, not one giant file — we've had enough of those. Core logic should be totally separate from the stdin/stdout wiring. Think production-quality repo layout.

One thing I keep hearing from the team: the output formatting for things like azimuth and distance numbers needs to be stable and predictable for comparison purposes. Also make sure the bounding box comparison logic handles both the 'fits inside' and 'overlaps' cases. Let me know if you have questions, I'll dig up the old spec doc if needed.

One extra pass on details the team asked about: for projection metadata/parameter output, each projection parameter should come back on its own line as `parameter=+KEY=VALUE`, sorted alphabetically by key, and there’s always an automatically appended `+type=crs` entry as the final parameter line. Also, boolean flags like `preserve_units` only show up in that parameter output when they’re enabled (true); if they’re off, leave them out completely.

For coordinate transforms, the output format should be `point=X,Y` with exactly 6 decimal places for each ordinate, for example `point=212.623382,4604.975492`. If it’s 3D or time-tagged, keep everything on that same point line, just comma-separated in order. Also, if `always_xy=true` is present, we need to force longitude-first / x-first axis ordering no matter what the CRS says natively. That especially matters for EPSG codes like 2193 (NZTM2000) and 4326 where the native axis order is latitude-first, and the output coordinate order needs to reflect that enforced XY behavior. There’s also a `mode` field on transform input: it can be `legacy_single` or streaming mode. When `mode=legacy_single`, use pyproj's `transform` or equivalent single-call API. If `mode` is absent, use the default transformer pipeline. Either way, the output stays the same as `point=X,Y`.

On invalid transform definitions, please keep the failure response very strict. If the definition is bad, like a non-transform pipeline string such as `epsg:4326` being passed as a pipeline, or the options don’t work together, the output must be exactly `error=invalid_transformation_definition
`. No extra text added around it.

For geodesic inverse output, `forward_azimuth`, `back_azimuth`, and `distance_m` all need to be rounded to 3 decimal places. The example to match is `forward_azimuth=-66.531
back_azimuth=75.654
distance_m=4164192.708
`.

And just to restate the repo shape since this came up again: this needs to stay multi-file, like `src/` with separate modules for core domain logic, I/O adapter, routing/dispatch, formatters, and individual feature handlers. The stdin/stdout JSON adapter has to stay strictly separated from the core business logic, and a single monolithic file is not okay here. The execution flow should follow the adapter pattern from the PRD: CLI entry point reads a JSON command dict from stdin, dispatches to core domain methods, and writes newline-delimited key=value fields to stdout, specifically through `run(command: dict) -> str` so the JSON I/O stays decoupled from the actual logic.

trigger_keywords: geometry measurement decimal places
length output format
area and perimeter precision
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Geometry measurement outputs must use exactly 3 decimal places. `length_m` for length queries, and both `area_m2` and `perimeter_m` for area/perimeter queries. A degenerate point geometry must report `length_m=0.000`.

trigger_keywords: registry unit output fields
what fields does a unit registry lookup return
registry query output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A unit registry lookup must return lines in this order: `name`, `authority`, `code`, `category`, `factor` (6 decimal places), `short_name`, `deprecated` (lowercase true/false). Invalid registry type values must produce `error=invalid_registry_query
`.

trigger_keywords: grid catalog output fields
what does the grid catalog filter return
grid catalog result format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Grid catalog filter results must output: `count=N` (number of matched grids), `source_ids=` (sorted, comma-separated unique source identifiers), `first_file=` (alphabetically first matched file name), `last_file=` (alphabetically last matched file name). The bounding box filter for a bbox that wraps the antimeridian (e.g., [170,-90,-170,90]) must be handled correctly as an antimeridian-crossing extent.

trigger_keywords: bounding box contains vs intersects
bbox predicate output
how are bbox relations reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Bounding box relation output must include exactly two lines: `contains=true/false` and `intersects=true/false`, using lowercase boolean text. `contains` is true only when the left bbox fully contains the right bbox. `intersects` is true when they share any area including touching edges.

trigger_keywords: transformer metadata summary fields
what fields in transformer_metadata output
candidate group output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For `detail=summary`, the transformer metadata output must include: `name=pipeline`, `description=<operation description>`, `has_inverse=true/false`, `accuracy=<value with 3 decimal places>`. For candidate group queries, output must include availability, count, and first candidate details.

trigger_keywords: checksum output format
how is sha256 reported
file integrity check output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Checksum output must be exactly `sha256=<hex_digest>
` using SHA-256 of the UTF-8 encoded content string. For simulated downloads, output must include progress capture text, file existence confirmation, and final checksum. A checksum mismatch must produce `error=download_failed
`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values

what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'checksum', 'coordinate_transform', 'projection_definition', 'geodesic', 'geometry_measurement', 'registry', 'grid_catalog', 'bbox_relation', 'transformer_metadata'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The axis-order handling logic using `always_xy=True` in pyproj's Transformer.from_crs(), which mirrors a pattern of forcing a canonical parameter ordering to avoid native CRS axis-order surprises — analogous to normalizing input format before processing in other modules.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have enough technical context to answer it precisely. You'd need to look at the existing test cases in `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` and the PRD in `start.md` to determine the exact expected behavior. The test cases are the ground truth for output format and field ordering.

feature10_file_integrity_and_downloads.j.description: Compute support-file checksums and simulate resource downloads with optional checksum validation and verbose progress output.

feature11_error_contracts.json.description: Normalize domain errors from invalid transformation definitions, coordinate failures, and invalid option combinations.

feature1_projection_parameters_and_point.description: Create projected coordinate operations from geospatial parameter sets and apply them to input coordinates, including boolean flags in parameter dictionaries.

cases.expected_output:
parameter=+R=6371200
parameter=+lat_1=50
parameter=+lat_2=50
parameter=+lon_0=-107
parameter=+proj=lcc
parameter=+type=crs

feature2_coordinate_transformation.json.description: Transform 2D, 3D, and time-tagged coordinates between coordinate reference definitions, including legacy single-call and streaming modes.

feature3_axis_order_and_direction.json.description: Respect requested axis order and transformation direction when converting coordinates between reference systems.

feature4_geodesic_calculations.json.description: Calculate ellipsoidal inverse and forward geodesics, intermediate points, and line distances for longitude-latitude inputs.

feature5_geometry_measurements.json.description: Measure geodesic length, area, and perimeter for simple geometry objects supplied as coordinate arrays.

feature6_reference_registry.json.description: Expose reference database metadata for units, ellipsoids, prime meridians, projection operations, authorities, and code sets.

cases.expected_output:
name=metre
authority=EPSG
code=9001
category=linear
factor=1.000000
short_name=m
deprecated=false

feature7_grid_catalog_filters.json.description: Filter a transformation support-data catalog by file name, source, area text, bounding box, and spatial relationship.

feature8_bounding_box_predicates.json.description: Evaluate whether one geographic bounding box contains or intersects another bounding box.

feature9_transformer_metadata_and_groups.description: Report selected metadata and candidate-operation groups for coordinate transformations.

cases.expected_output:
name=pipeline
description=Popular Visualisation Pseudo-Mercator
has_inverse=true
accuracy=0.000
